attributes.stem:
A four-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of fever, lethargy, and occasional headaches. He attends childcare 4 days per week and last week most of the class were absent due to cough and cold symptoms. His mother reports that he seems to feel worse every second day with temperatures reaching 38.5°C, followed by sweating. The child returned from visiting his home in Papua New Guinea two weeks ago. His medical history includes frequent croup though no hospital admissions and his parents prefer to use traditional medicine however the daycare insisted for a GP review. He is unvaccinated and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears mildly unwell but alert, with a pulse rate of 110/min, blood pressure 100/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. He has subtle pallor and a spleen tip just palpable below the costal margin. There is no neck stiffness, photophobia, or rash. Cardiorespiratory examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most essential next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents an unvaccinated child with fever, lethargy, and headaches following travel to Papua New Guinea, a malaria-endemic region. The cyclical fever pattern occurring every 48 hours is highly suggestive of malaria, particularly Plasmodium vivax or Plasmodium falciparum, though the later typically presents within the first month of exposure. The presence of splenomegaly and history of incomplete prophylaxis further support this diagnosis.

Thick and thin blood films remain the gold standard for diagnosing malaria. Thick films allow for detection of parasites, while thin films enable species identification and quantification of parasitaemia. This is crucial for determining appropriate treatment, as P. falciparum can cause severe, life-threatening malaria, particularly in children. Blood films should be repeated at least three times over 12-24 hours if initial tests are negative but clinical suspicion remains high to exclude malaria. It is unclear in this question whether the patient had malaria prophylaxis though it may be unlikely if the parents only use traditional medicine. In one study, up to 30% of malaria deaths were in patients who were taking malaria prophylaxis (though this includes those with poor compliance and delayed presentation). Therefore one cannot assume that a patient does not have malaria based on the the history of taking antimalaria prophylaxis.

Measles serology might be considered given the child is unvaccinated, which puts him at higher risk for vaccine-preventable diseases. However, the clinical picture does not align with measles, which typically presents with coryza, conjunctivitis, cough, and a characteristic rash that spreads from the head downward. The absence of a rash and the presence of cyclical fevers with splenomegaly make measles less likely than malaria in this scenario.

Nasopharyngeal PCR swab would be appropriate for investigating respiratory viral infections, which are common in childcare settings. While the child's classmates had respiratory symptoms, his cyclical fever pattern, recent travel to a malaria-endemic area, and splenomegaly are more consistent with malaria than a typical viral upper respiratory tract infection. Though an upper respiratory tract infection from daycare is very likely, given the potential severity of untreated malaria especially in children, investigating for malaria takes precedence.

Blood culture is important for detecting bacteraemia, which can coexist with malaria or present similarly. However, given the clinical presentation and travel history, malaria testing should take precedence. Blood cultures would be appropriate as part of a septic workup but would not be the most essential first investigation in this scenario.

Lumbar puncture would be indicated if there were signs of meningitis such as neck stiffness, photophobia, or altered consciousness which is not present in this scenario. While cerebral malaria can cause neurological symptoms, this child does not exhibit specific signs of meningitis or encephalitis that would warrant an immediate lumbar puncture. The priority is to diagnose and treat malaria, which can rapidly progress to severe disease in children.

Whilst fevers are a common presentation for viral illnesses transmitted in daycare situations, it is essential for immediate treatment and for public health safety to rule out malaria in a febrile returned traveler.
learningPoints: Malaria should be considered in any febrile child with a history of travel to endemic regions, even if prophylaxis was taken.
Plasmodium falciparum malaria can progress rapidly in children and requires urgent diagnosis and treatment.
Thick and thin blood films remain the gold standard for diagnosis of malaria, allowing species identification and quantification of parasitaemia.
references: Leggat P.  Assessment of febrile illness in the returned traveler.  Australian Family Physician. 2007: 36 (5).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/54226dae-628f-487b-8029-d5dadc43b346/attachment.aspx

Benson J. Malaria in the Australian refugee population. Australian Family Physician. 2007: 38 (8).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/99d8763f-7cfa-493f-8ee3-d09bbcf5796a/attachment.aspx

Manas Kotepui, Kwuntida Uthaisar Kotepui, Frederick Ramirez Masangkay, Polrat Wilairatana. Evidence of malarial chemoprophylaxis among travellers who died from malaria: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Malaria Journal [Internet]. 2023 Nov 25 [cited 2024 Oct 10];22(1). Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38001503/
